title: mechanical
main_content: we hypothesized that mechanical forces exerted on the cell/nucleus may cause stable changes in gene expression and tumour cell behaviour. To test this hypothesis,
context:

outcome:
we adapted a system to confine human mela-
noma cells (A375 cell line) in vitro at predefined heights (3 µm) using a
polydimethylsiloxane piston and micropatterned coverslips13 (Fig. 1l).
predicted_context: It is established that physical confinement, such as a cancer cell migrating through a dense extracellular matrix, induces severe cellular and nuclear deformation. This mechanical stress on the nucleus stretches the nuclear envelope, uncouples chromatin from the nuclear lamina, and alters chromatin architecture, thereby driving transcriptional reprogramming and altering cell state. Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic devices engineered with highly defined spatial geometries (e.g., narrow microchannels) are a standard, reproducible in vitro model used to physically confine cells. This system allows for the precise isolation and application of mechanical compressive forces independent of biochemical cues from the tumor microenvironment.
predicted_outcome: we will pass human melanoma cell lines (e.g., A375) through PDMS microfluidic devices containing either narrow, confining microchannels (3 μm width) or wide, unconfined control channels (15 μm width). Following passage, we will isolate the cells and perform bulk RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) to profile stable transcriptomic changes, ATAC-seq to assess mechanically-induced shifts in chromatin accessibility, and Boyden chamber transwell assays to evaluate subsequent shifts toward an invasive phenotype.
